title: MCP Stack Registry - 9 Pre-Built Stacks
description: Curated MCP server combinations optimized for specific use cases. Each stack contains 4 servers selected for maximum synergy and minimal conflict.

stackCuratorAlgorithm.name: MCP Stack Curator v10
stackCuratorAlgorithm.description: Algorithmic approach to optimal 4-server MCP stack composition

principles.name: Complementarity
principles.description: Each server must provide a capability the other 3 cannot. No two servers should solve the same problem.

principles.name: Data Flow
principles.description: Servers must form a clear data pipeline: extract → process → store → verify. At least one server must produce output another consumes.

principles.name: Universal Base
principles.description: Memory MCP and Filesystem MCP are universal enhancers — they can join any stack without conflict. Only include when they actively improve the data flow.

principles.name: Conflict Avoidance
principles.description: Never combine Postgres + Supabase, two web search servers, or two file-writers on the same directory. These create race conditions and data conflicts.

principles.name: Minimum Surprise
principles.description: All 4 servers must be free, publicly available, and have active maintenance (commits within 6 months). No private or paywalled servers in pre-built stacks.

conflictMatrix.Postgres + Supabase: CRITICAL - Both modify database schema, creates migration conflicts
conflictMatrix.Brave Search + Serper: WARNING - Two web search servers produce redundant results
conflictMatrix.Filesystem + Filesystem: CRITICAL - Two file writers on same paths creates race conditions
conflictMatrix.GitHub + GitLab: CONDITIONAL - Only compatible if managing repos on separate platforms
conflictMatrix.Postgres + SQLite: COMPATIBLE - Postgres for production, SQLite for local analytics is fine
conflictMatrix.Memory + any server: COMPATIBLE - Memory MCP is a universal complement

synergyMatrix.Memory + any: Universal enhancer — stores preferences, context, and learned patterns
synergyMatrix.Sequential Thinking + any complex task: Structured reasoning before action reduces errors 40%
synergyMatrix.Filesystem + any artifact producer: Saves generated content to organized project structure
synergyMatrix.Brave Search + Fetch: Search finds sources, Fetch extracts full content — classic research pipeline
synergyMatrix.PictoFlux AI + Filesystem: Generate images, save to project assets — visual content pipeline
synergyMatrix.GitHub + Filesystem: Code changes tracked in git, files managed locally — development workflow
synergyMatrix.Brave Search + Sequential Thinking: Research findings structured into logical arguments
synergyMatrix.Postgres + Filesystem: Database exports to local files for backup and analysis

curatorRules.description: The MCP Stack Curator recommends which 4 servers to use together. Rules: (1) No two servers with identical capabilities, (2) Pipeline bonus when servers form a clear data flow, (3) Complementary bonus when servers cover different layers, (4) Overlap penalty when servers have competing features.

stacks.name: Full-Stack Web Studio
stacks.description: Build, deploy, and monitor production web applications end-to-end
stacks.useCase: Solo dev or small team shipping a full-stack Next.js app from concept to production

servers.name: filesystem
servers.reason: Read/write project files — the foundation of any code project

servers.name: github
servers.reason: PR management, issue tracking, CI/CD pipeline triggers

servers.name: vercel
servers.reason: Deploy previews on every PR, production deploys on merge

servers.name: postgres
servers.reason: Database migrations, seed data, query debugging

synergy.reason: Filesystem + GitHub covers code lifecycle; Vercel handles deployment; Postgres provides persistent storage. Zero overlap.

stacks.curatorNotes: GitHub handles version control, Postgres manages production data, Filesystem edits project files locally, and Sequential Thinking ensures architectural decisions are reasoned through before code is written.
stacks.dataFlow: Plan architecture → Edit code (Filesystem) → Version control (GitHub) → Deploy (Vercel) → Manage data (Postgres)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses production-grade database but gains lighter local analytics with no server setup
Loses managed deployment but gains containerized builds

stacks.name: AI Research Lab
stacks.description: Research, analyze, and synthesize information with AI-powered search and knowledge graphs
stacks.useCase: Deep research workflows requiring multi-source verification and structured knowledge extraction

servers.name: brave-search
servers.reason: Web search for real-time information discovery

servers.name: fetch
servers.reason: Retrieve and extract content from discovered URLs

servers.name: memory
servers.reason: Persist research findings across sessions as structured knowledge

servers.name: sqlite
servers.reason: Store and query structured research data locally

synergy.reason: Search discovers, Fetch extracts, Memory persists, SQLite structures. Clean pipeline with zero overlap.

stacks.curatorNotes: Search (Brave) and extraction (Fetch) form the classic research pipeline, Arxiv adds academic depth that web search cannot reach, and Memory ensures no finding is lost across sessions.
stacks.dataFlow: Search sources (Brave) → Extract content (Fetch) → Academic depth (Arxiv) → Index findings (Memory)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses local SQL analytics but gains academic paper access
Loses live web scraping but gains ability to save and organize research locally as files

stacks.name: Content Engine
stacks.description: End-to-end content creation, optimization, and distribution pipeline
stacks.useCase: Content teams producing SEO-optimized articles, social posts, and newsletters at scale

servers.name: filesystem
servers.reason: Read briefs, write drafts, save final content

servers.name: brave-search
servers.reason: Topic research, competitor analysis, trending queries

servers.name: google-drive
servers.reason: Access brand guidelines, editorial calendars, asset libraries

servers.name: slack
servers.reason: Content approvals, team notifications, review workflows

synergy.reason: Search + Filesystem covers research-to-draft; Google Drive provides source material; Slack enables human-in-the-loop review.

mismatches.warning: Both store files — decide primary storage to avoid split-brain

stacks.curatorNotes: Brave Search researches topics, PictoFlux creates featured images, Filesystem organizes published content, and Memory preserves brand voice so every piece sounds consistent.
stacks.dataFlow: Research topics (Brave Search) → Generate visuals (PictoFlux) → Store content (Filesystem) → Recall brand voice (Memory)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses cloud collaboration but gains persistent memory of pipeline configurations and schema decisions
Loses web research but gains structured content outlining for long-form writing

stacks.name: DevOps Command Center
stacks.description: Deploy, monitor, and respond to incidents across cloud infrastructure
stacks.useCase: Platform teams managing multi-service deployments with observability and incident response

servers.name: github
servers.reason: Code changes, CI/CD triggers, deployment workflows

servers.name: docker
servers.reason: Container management, image builds, compose orchestration

servers.name: kubernetes
servers.reason: Cluster management, scaling, rolling deployments

servers.name: slack
servers.reason: Incident alerts, deployment notifications, runbook access

synergy.reason: GitHub -> Docker -> K8s is the standard build-ship-run pipeline. Slack adds the human notification layer.

stacks.curatorNotes: GitHub audits remote code, Filesystem scans local code, Brave Search looks up known CVEs, and Sequential Thinking systematically models threat vectors — full coverage from code to context to reasoning.
stacks.dataFlow: Code changes (GitHub) → Container builds (Docker) → Orchestration (K8s) → Notifications (Slack)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses cluster orchestration but gains observability dashboards and metrics
Loses container management but gains infrastructure-as-code provisioning

stacks.name: Data Pipeline Studio
stacks.description: Ingest, transform, analyze, and visualize data at any scale
stacks.useCase: Data engineers building ETL pipelines, running analytics, and creating dashboards

servers.name: postgres
servers.reason: Source and destination for relational data

servers.name: sqlite
servers.reason: Local analysis, prototyping, lightweight queries

servers.name: fetch
servers.reason: Pull data from APIs and web sources

servers.name: filesystem
servers.reason: Read CSVs, write reports, manage data artifacts

synergy.reason: Fetch + Filesystem for ingestion; Postgres for production data; SQLite for analysis sandbox. Clean separation of concerns.

mismatches.warning: Both are SQL databases — use Postgres for production, SQLite for local analysis only

stacks.curatorNotes: Fetch extracts raw data from the web, Filesystem stages intermediate ETL files, Postgres serves as the production warehouse, and SQLite provides lightweight local analytics — a complete data pipeline with no overlapping roles.
stacks.dataFlow: Extract data (Fetch) → Stage files (Filesystem) → Warehouse (Postgres) → Analyze locally (SQLite)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses local SQL analytics but gains persistent memory of pipeline configurations and schema decisions
Loses direct data scraping but gains broader discovery of data sources and APIs

stacks.name: Security Audit Toolkit
stacks.description: Scan, analyze, and remediate security vulnerabilities across code and infrastructure
stacks.useCase: Security teams running vulnerability assessments, dependency audits, and compliance checks

servers.name: filesystem
servers.reason: Read source code, config files, dependency manifests

servers.name: github
servers.reason: Access repos, check branch protections, review security advisories

servers.name: brave-search
servers.reason: Look up CVEs, security advisories, exploit databases

servers.name: docker
servers.reason: Scan container images for vulnerabilities

synergy.reason: Filesystem + GitHub covers code security; Search covers external threat intel; Docker covers container security.

stacks.curatorNotes: GitHub scans remote code, Filesystem scans local code, Brave Search looks up known CVEs, and Docker covers container security — full coverage from code to context to reasoning.
stacks.dataFlow: Scan remote code (GitHub) → Scan local code (Filesystem) → Lookup CVEs (Brave Search) → Scan containers (Docker)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses container scanning but gains structured threat modeling
Loses broad CVE search but gains ability to pull detailed vulnerability reports from specific advisory pages

stacks.name: Creative Studio
stacks.description: Design, generate, and manage visual assets with AI-powered creative tools
stacks.useCase: Designers and creators producing images, UI mockups, and visual content at scale

servers.name: filesystem
servers.reason: Save generated images, organize asset libraries

servers.name: fetch
servers.reason: Download reference images, pull design inspiration

servers.name: google-drive
servers.reason: Share creative assets with team, access brand kits

servers.name: slack
servers.reason: Creative review workflows, design critique notifications

synergy.reason: Filesystem for local work; Fetch for sourcing; Google Drive for sharing; Slack for review. Good creative pipeline.

mismatches.warning: Asset storage split — use Drive for shared, filesystem for local

stacks.curatorNotes: These four servers cover the full creative pipeline: Fetch supplies design specs, Filesystem persists assets, Google Drive shares with team, and Slack enables human-in-the-loop review.
stacks.dataFlow: Fetch references → Filesystem saves assets → Google Drive shares → Slack reviews

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses deep content extraction but gains broader trend research for inspiration-first workflows
Loses team review workflows but gains persistent memory of brand preferences

stacks.name: Mobile App Workshop
stacks.description: Design, develop, test, and ship React Native mobile applications
stacks.useCase: Mobile developers building cross-platform apps with React Native and backend APIs

servers.name: filesystem
servers.reason: Read/write app code, manage project structure

servers.name: github
servers.reason: Version control, PR reviews, release management

servers.name: fetch
servers.reason: API testing, backend data integration

servers.name: memory
servers.reason: Persist app architecture decisions and API contracts

synergy.reason: Filesystem + GitHub for code lifecycle; Fetch for API integration; Memory for architectural decisions across sessions.

stacks.curatorNotes: Filesystem handles local app code, GitHub manages version control, Fetch tests backend APIs, and Memory persists architectural decisions — a complete mobile development workflow.
stacks.dataFlow: Write app code (Filesystem) → Version control (GitHub) → Test APIs (Fetch) → Store decisions (Memory)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses persistent decisions but gains containerized test environments
Loses general API testing but gains backend-as-a-service with auth and realtime

stacks.name: Unified AI Gateway
stacks.description: 9Router + OWL Proxy Defense + 4-CLI unified gateway — route AI requests through free-tier providers first, with circuit breaker fallback and protocol-first proxy defense
stacks.useCase: Teams and power users managing multiple AI providers (Gemini, Claude, Codex, OpenCode) with unified routing, proxy defense, and credential vault

servers.name: filesystem
servers.reason: Read/write 9Router configs, proxy routing rules, and credential vault files

servers.name: github
servers.reason: Auto-commit proxy config changes, version control for routing rules

servers.name: docker
servers.reason: Run 9Router gateway, sing-box proxy, and Redis cache containers

servers.name: fetch
servers.reason: Health check proxy endpoints, test provider connectivity, monitor token validity

synergy.reason: Filesystem stores configs; GitHub versions them; Docker runs the stack; Fetch monitors health. Complete infrastructure lifecycle with zero overlap.

stacks.curatorNotes: This is the canonical AI infrastructure stack: Filesystem handles local configuration, GitHub provides version control, Docker runs the gateway and proxy containers, and Fetch monitors health endpoints.
stacks.dataFlow: Store configs (Filesystem) → Version control (GitHub) → Run stack (Docker) → Monitor health (Fetch)

alternatives.tradeoff: Loses simple container orchestration but gains enterprise-grade scaling
Loses health monitoring but gains ability to search for new AI providers and models
